claims.statement: The value of a quantity is expressed as the product of a numerical value and a unit, so the same quantity can have different numerical values when expressed in different units.
claims.scope: Introductory treatment of scalar physical quantities and coherent unit representations.
claims.uncertainty: This distinction does not address measurement uncertainty or vector direction.

claims.statement: A ratio of equal quantity values can serve as a multiplicative conversion factor equal to one, changing the numerical value and unit representation without changing the quantity.
claims.scope: Exact unit equalities stated in the lesson's mathematical tasks.
claims.uncertainty: Empirical or rounded conversion factors may introduce uncertainty outside this lesson's exact definitions.

claims.statement: A quantity equation is independent of the units used to express its quantities, whereas an equation among numerical values depends on the chosen units.
claims.scope: Quantity equations and numerical-value equations in introductory quantitative science.
claims.uncertainty: Dimensional consistency does not establish that an equation is the correct model.

claims.statement: A quantity dimension expresses dependence on base quantities as powers while omitting numerical factors; quantities with different dimensions are different kinds of quantity.
claims.scope: Introductory scalar quantity calculus in the International System of Quantities.
claims.uncertainty: Quantities with the same dimension are not necessarily the same kind, and dimension does not encode scalar, vector, or tensor character.

claims.statement: A proposed quantity relation that adds or equates incompatible dimensions is invalid, but matching dimensions cannot prove the relation because dimension omits numerical factors and quantities of the same dimension need not be the same kind.
claims.scope: Dimensional screening of introductory quantity equations and arithmetic operations.
claims.uncertainty: Dimensional consistency cannot select a coefficient, sign, causal model, boundary condition, or empirically correct relation.

claims.statement: When a unit is raised to a power, a unit conversion must be applied to that full power so every occurrence of the original unit cancels.
claims.scope: Algebraic conversions of squared and cubed units using exact linear equalities.
claims.uncertainty: This does not address affine temperature-scale conversions, which cannot generally be handled as simple multiplicative factors.

claims.statement: Clear quantity notation associates numerical values unambiguously with their unit symbols and places a space between a numerical value and most unit symbols.
claims.scope: SI-style writing used in this lesson, with the documented exceptions for plane-angle symbols outside scope.
claims.uncertainty: Discipline-specific typography and non-SI style guides can impose additional conventions.
